title: Avoid complex-extractions anti-pattern when refactoring a 4-arg function
prompt:
Given:

```
def process_order(order, user, config, session) do
  total = order.subtotal + order.tax + order.shipping
  name = user.first_name <> " " <> user.last_name
  region = config.shipping_regions[order.region_id]
  session_id = session.id
  MyApp.Orders.finalize(order.id, name, region, total, session_id)
end
```

You could destructure all four args in the function head, but that creates the 'complex extractions in clauses' anti-pattern. Instead, do LIGHT pattern matching (match on order and session at the boundary with `%Order{id: order_id}` and `%Session{id: session_id}`) and leave the deeper extractions in the body. Output a complete `defmodule MyApp.OrderProcessor do ... end`.

must_not_contain: %Order{subtotal: subtotal, tax: tax, shipping: shipping, region_id: region_id, id: order_id}

tier_rationale: Explicit test of avoiding the complex-extractions anti-pattern. Sonnet tends to over-destructure when told to 'use pattern matching'.
research_citation: Elixir anti-patterns guide + Elixir Streams: 'Refactoring an anti-pattern: complex extractions in function heads'
